name: War Games 9/11
description: The "War Games 9/11" theory posits that the terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001, were either facilitated, allowed to happen, or even orchestrated by elements within the United States government, military, or intelligence agencies. A central argument revolves around the numerous war games and military exercises that were taking place on and around September 11th. Proponents suggest that these exercises created confusion, drained resources, and provided cover for the actual attacks, effectively hindering the ability of the military and air defenses to respond effectively. The theory gained traction in the years following 9/11, fueled by skepticism about the official narrative, perceived inconsistencies in the 9/11 Commission Report, and a general distrust of government. Common arguments include the alleged stand-down orders given to interceptors, the simulation of similar attack scenarios in pre-9/11 exercises, and the perceived lack of accountability for failures in air defense. Mainstream perspectives dismiss these claims as unfounded conspiracy theories, arguing that the war games were routine training exercises and that any perceived failures in response were due to the unprecedented nature of the attacks and the limitations of existing air defense protocols. Critiques also point to the lack of concrete evidence supporting the claims of intentional sabotage or orchestration, and emphasize the extensive investigations and reports that have debunked many of the specific allegations made by proponents.
subtitle: Military exercises and the 9/11 attacks.

claims.claim: War games provided cover for the attacks.
claims.evidence: Multiple military exercises were ongoing on 9/11, including Vigilant Warrior, Northern Vigilance, and Global Guardian, simulating scenarios similar to the attacks.
claims.criticism: These exercises were routine and planned well in advance. The simultaneous nature of the attacks overwhelmed existing protocols and resources, but there's no evidence of intentional manipulation.

claims.claim: Stand-down orders prevented interception of the hijacked planes.
claims.evidence: Claims that interceptor aircraft were deliberately delayed or prevented from engaging the hijacked planes.
claims.criticism: Air defense protocols required positive identification of hostile aircraft before interception. The rapid unfolding of events and the lack of prior warning made timely interception extremely difficult, not due to deliberate orders but procedural delays and the unprecedented nature of the attacks.

claims.claim: Pre-9/11 exercises simulated similar attacks, indicating prior knowledge.
claims.evidence: Exercises like Vigilant Guardian involved hypothetical scenarios of hijacked planes used as weapons.
claims.criticism: The fact that these scenarios were considered in training exercises demonstrates awareness of potential threats, not foreknowledge of the specific attacks. Such exercises are designed to prepare for various contingencies.

keyFigures.name: David Ray Griffin
keyFigures.role: Prominent 9/11 conspiracy theorist who wrote extensively on the topic.

keyFigures.name: Richard Clarke
keyFigures.role: Counter-terrorism expert who served in the Clinton and Bush administrations; his testimony before the 9/11 Commission refuted many conspiracy theories.

timeline.event: Terrorist attacks on the World Trade Center and the Pentagon.
Emergence and spread of 9/11 conspiracy theories, including the War Games theory.

sources.author: David Ray Griffin
sources.work: The New Pearl Harbor: Disturbing Questions About the Bush Administration and 9/11
sources.title: David Ray Griffin

sources.author: Philip Shenon
sources.work: The Commission: The Uncensored History of the 9/11 Investigation
